serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of stir-fried green beans is approximately 150 grams.

nutrient_source.title: Green beans, stir-fried, with oil
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are primarily from USDA FoodData Central for 'Green beans, stir-fried, with oil' (FDC ID 1105378). Sodium content has been adapted upward to approximately 380 mg per 100 g to reflect typical Chinese stir-fry preparation with soy sauce and seasonings.

gi_source.title: Green beans
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database for 'green beans' (typically GI 15–30); no specific entry for stir-fried green beans is available. The low GI reflects the high dietary fibre and low digestible carbohydrate content of green beans. GL per serving calculated as (25 × 6.0 × 1.5) / 100 = 2.25.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasonings used in Chinese stir-fry preparation.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume stir-fried green beans in moderation due to the elevated sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g) from soy sauce; home preparation with low-sodium soy sauce or reduced seasoning is recommended.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 25) and high dietary fibre content of green beans.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried green beans are a suitable vegetable side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their low glycemic index and fibre content, which support postprandial blood glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and fat contribution from cooking oil.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular health, individuals should be mindful of the sodium content and choose preparations using minimal oil and low-sodium soy sauce to reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Green Beans, known in Chinese as 干煸四季豆 (Gan Bian Si Ji Dou), is a classic Sichuan dish in which green beans are dry-fried in a wok until slightly wrinkled and blistered, concentrating their natural sweetness and creating a tender-crisp texture. They are then tossed with aromatics such as garlic, ginger, dried chilies, and Sichuan peppercorns, delivering the characteristic spicy and numbing mala flavour profile. Soy sauce provides umami depth and saltiness. Nutritionally, the dish is relatively low in calories (~68 kcal per 100 g) and carbohydrates (~6 g per 100 g), with a meaningful dietary fibre content (~2.7 g per 100 g) that supports digestive health. The glycemic index is low (approximately 25), making it a suitable vegetable side for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate to high (~380 mg per 100 g) due to soy sauce, which warrants attention for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegetarian and vegan-friendly and is commonly served as a side dish in Chinese restaurants across Singapore and globally.
